question: What is the role of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS)?

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components in the NuScale reactor, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the specific alloys and material specifications for the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, heater sheaths, and bolting.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat following reactor shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours to protect fuel and system integrity, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

edges.description: Section A provides detailed material specifications for the pressurizer, which is identified in Section B as an integral component of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), thereby specifying the construction details for a subsystem described more generally in Section B.

Section A describes the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is part of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) that the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in Section B is designed to cool during certain events, connecting them through their roles in the RCS.

Section B describes the Decay Heat Removal System's function to remove residual and decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), which is summarized in Section A as the system responsible for primary coolant circulation, thus Section B specifies a subsystem function that supports the overall purpose described in Section A.
